data.title: DBV Technologies: A Tough Nut To Crack In A Shrinking Market
data.description:
DBV Technologies Overview

DBV Technologies’ stock (DBVT) is up over 400% in the past year.

Almost exactly a year ago today, DBV announced that the FDA informed them that its VITESSE Phase 3 trial evaluating its Viaskin Peanut patch in 4 to 7-year-olds would, alone, be sufficient for a BLA.

Months later, in December, DBV revealed VITESSE topline results.

46.6% of children treated with the VIASKIN® Peanut patch met response criteria at 12 months, compared to 14.8% of children in the placebo arm.

Its plan was to submit a BLA for 4 to 7 year-olds in the 1H 2026.

Having covered Aimmune (later acquired by Nestle in 2020 for Palforzia, a peanut allergen oral immunotherapy powder) in the past, I have some familiarity with this market.

Below, I make the case that DBVT’s ~$1B market capitalization merits caution ahead of what figures to be a challenging launch for Viaskin.

A Shrinking Market

Peanut allergy management primarily focuses on avoidance and the treatment of accidental exposures.

Over the past decade, there has been a shift to peanut allergy prevention.

In 2015, the LEAP study proved that early introduction reduced allergy risk by >80%. And by 2021, guidelines around the world recommended early introduction for all infants.

The 2025 iREACH trial showed that baseline adoption of these new recommendations was just ~10%-35%.

Current data (conducted by CHOP and published in Pediatrics) shows a 43% drop in overall peanut allergy incidence since the 2017 guidelines were implemented.

The study found significant reductions in the prevalence of peanut IgE-mediated food allergy (from 0.79% to 0.45% of the study population) and any IgE-mediated food allergy (1.46% to 0.93% of the population) from the time before the guidelines to after the addendum guidelines were introduced.

It has been almost a decade since the NIAID/NIH addendum. So, in just this short time, DBV’s target market has materially diminished. And it’s not like DBV can lean on the millions of adults that have peanut allergies.

Mind you, DBV has been developing its "peanut patch" since ~2013. Recall that its first BLA was rejected in 2020 over adhesion concerns. The company then had to redesign the patch and test it again.

So, DBV’s current estimates (see slide below), in my view, appear off since they are using outdated data from 2018.

Here are my estimates for Viaskin’s US target population based on recent data:

A major subjective assumption in the table above is the -2.0% annual population change. This actually may be too generous. And, as also detailed above, Viaskin will also face competition with Xolair and off-label oral immunotherapies.

Now, after acquiring Palforzia for $2.6B, Nestle sold it to a Swiss company for pennies on the dollar.

Prior, Nestle had to incur >$2B in impairment charges due to Palforzia’s underperformance on the market. Some attributed this to COVID, some to low demand.

Palforzia also required a REMS and its oral administration caused GI side effects. Viaskin’s transdermal administration should avoid both issues. But were these Palforzia’s only downfalls?

Many did not see the cost benefit of Palforzia (~$10k) versus supermarket peanut flour, allergist oversight, and a kitchen scale.

A lot of parents also opt for “Strict Avoidance + EpiPen” in favor of the daily risks of incremental exposure. And it was just announced that its current owner is axing Palforzia altogether.

While Viaskin is a major administration route advantage over Palforzia, its lower efficacy could blur its value.

Palforzia was very effective, but wasn't well-tolerated. Viaskin looks like a “Low-Bar, High-Safety” product.

And now there’s Novartis’ (NVS) Xolair, which is quite effective for peanut allergies. It’s also effective for other allergies (i.e., eggs and milk).

Guess what? 75.5% of children with at least one food allergy have multiple concurrent food allergies. Among peanut-allergic kids, there is a high co-occurrence with tree nuts. Xolair, approved in 2024, covers multiple foods at once.

Not to trivialize allergies, but using Viaskin over Xolair, for instance, when there are multiple allergies is akin to using a few different remote controllers (Viaskin, and so on) when a universal remote exists (Xolair).

Viaskin may still retain a niche use case.

But Viaskin's actual TAM is much smaller than what outdated prevalence figures suggest.

Then there’s Novartis' remibrutinib (Phase 3-ready; already FDA-approved as Rhapsido for urticaria), which could become a major competitor in a couple of years from now. Promising Phase 2 data was just published in JACI.

Pharma DCF

Starting with Target Pop. funnels:

The prevalence figures from both peanut and milk-EoE are well-cited. For milk, the vast majority of kids outgrow their milk sensitivity. The 0.5% is referring to the persistent “IgE mediated” group.

"Diagnosed & Seeking Care" removes the families who opt for a state of strict avoidance (more than you think) or are not managed by an allergist (primary care physicians are unlikely to be the ones prescribing Viaskin).

For Peak Share, I think I modeled generously: 25% for all three indications. 25% doesn’t sound like much if you are unfamiliar with drug modeling, but it often denotes a clear market leader.

Zeroing in on peanut (I have provided the milk/milk-EoE cards in the Appendix; both are still in early-stage development and do not contribute much to DBVT’s NPV), WACC is 12% (standard for a drug in NDA/BLA stage), Probability of Success is at 85% (a bit lower than typical, but need to account for patch risks), 2027 Launch, 2033 Peak (six years after Launch; standard), LOE 2039 (12 years for biologic), -2% annual Population Growth, $8k/year Net Price (a premium over Palforzia, which is likely ~$5k/year after rebates and discounts), 10% COGS (standard), 65% Launch OpEx (DBV's first product launch) for 3 years, 35% Steady-State OpEx (a bit higher than standard to account for high S&M costs), and a Competitor Entry Yr of 2029 (remibrutinib) with a 15% Competitive Impact (impacting market share).

Great! So I have Viaskin generating roughly $180M at peak and worth $251M in risk-adjusted NPV.

Unfortunately, due, primarily, to the nature of drug development (e.g., low odds of success in early-stage trials, launch still years away), Viaskin (milk) and Viaskin (milk-EoE) are negative rNPV assets until further de-risking (i.e., positive Phase 2 data).

With $288.2M in cash, it’s challenging to envision this company becoming profitable without getting ahold of more money in some way (i.e., equity raise). Launching their first ever product in peanut allergy and advancing Viaskin to late-stage trials in other indications will be costly. So my model notes a $101.8M Funding Gap…that can be filed by raising 5.7M new shares over the next three years at $18/share. DBV projects its cash runway extends “into the second quarter of 2027.”

Put everything together and I have that DBVT’s Intrinsic Equity Value is $378M. This is its Total Global Pipeline value plus cash.

The $7.82 Fair Value/Share estimate is derived from dividing its Intrinsic Equity Value and raise (the Total Equity Value, post-raise and fully funded) by its Diluted Share Count of 61.4M.

Given that DBVT’s current share price is $20, my Fair Value/Share estimate implies 60.9% downside.

Scenario Analysis

Because my results are so off from the current share price, I did a Scenario Analysis with a 40%/20%/40% Bear/Base/Bull weighting (I usually assign a greater weight to my Base case).

In my “Bear” case, the POS (on average; for the entire pipeline) drops from 68.8% to 58.5%. Peak Share falls under 10%. WACC rises to 12.8%. Net Price slips to $6k. DBV is worth $1.81/share.

In my “Bull” case, the POS falls just under 80%. Peak Share jumps to nearly 30%. WACC drops to 10.3%. Net Price increases to $12k. DBV is worth $24.39.

I end up with a probability-weighted valuation of $12.04/share, which comes much closer to DBVT’s current price than my “Base” case does.

Conclusion

Be it shrinking incidence, competition, or launch challenges in a prevention-success era (as exemplified by Palforzia), Viaskin has quite a few hurdles to clear in peanut allergy to become a viable product. These issues, alone, make me bearish on DBV. And I haven’t even addressed “potentially fatal” surprises like a REMS requirement or persistent adhesion issues leading to further delay.

Meanwhile, other indications, like milk and milk-EoE, are not compelling.

DBV appears materially overvalued (initiate DBVT with "Sell" rating).

Risks to Thesis

I understand that my bearish thesis may be overstating things.

I do see the appeal of Viaskin in pediatric peanut allergy (high safety + convenience), and favorable regulatory outcomes could cause the stock to appreciate further.

It is possible that I am underestimating the TAM or pricing power of Viaskin.

Finally, I may be underappreciating DBV’s patch technology, which could be attractive to big allergy players like Novartis and Sanofi (SNY).

As for a potential future re-rating, I want to see a clean BLA and a strong launch uptake.

Appendix

Viaskin (Milk)

Viaskin (Milk-EoE)

data.title: Fermi: Lack Of Tenant Progress Is Deeply Concerning
data.description:
Shares of Fermi Inc. (FRMI) have been a very poor performer since their IPO in the fall of 2025, losing about three-quarters of their value. Investors received more bad news Monday after the company reported weak results and limited progress in securing tenants for its aggressive data center buildout plans. When the company first went public, I was deeply skeptical, but I moved shares to a Hold rating in December after having seen a 70% drop since my Sell recommendation in October. In that article, I wrote I would simply “avoid Fermi,” but in hindsight, actively shorting shares would have been better. As an investor, it can be important not to be “greedy” and press winning trades too far; however, in this case, there was more downside to monetize with shares down 40% since December. With updated financials, now is a good time to revisit Fermi.

Fermi Has Yet to Sign a Tenant

In 2025, the company reported a GAAP loss of $1.13. As the company began operations on January 10th, 2025, this technically reflected a partial year of results, though it is close to a full year. Results included $442 million of non-cash charges; otherwise, its loss was closer to $45 million. Now, Fermi is essentially a pre-revenue company. It has no customers yet, and it instead needs to build out data centers and the power infrastructure to support them in order to win tenants and generate revenue.

As such, shares are not reacting so badly because the company is running at a loss; in my view, even the most bullish investor should recognize losses are likely in the near term. The question, however, is when the company will sign tenants and inflect to meaningful revenue, and here, the story is increasingly problematic. Fermi has yet to sign a tenant to a definitive lease, and its shareholder letter alongside results makes it seem like no such agreement is imminent.

Management wrote that it is being “deliberate and consistent” in seeking the right partner, terms, and capital structure. Because the first agreement will likely be used as a baseline for future tenants, it is something management “intends to get right." No one can really disagree with these principles; obviously, securing good terms with a creditworthy tenant is essential. The problem is that executing on this plan is proving to be very difficult.

No Deal Appears Imminent

Back in September, as the company was preparing to go public, Fermi had a non-binding letter of intent with an investment-grade partner to sign a lease. In November, there was a $150 million commitment to fund some infrastructure buildout before potential occupancy. Then in December, the tenant backed out of the deal before any funds were spent. While the letter of intent remains in place, it has always been nonbinding, and no signed lease with this counterparty seems likely given the cancellation of the November agreement.

Fermi says it is in discussions with other prospective tenants, but it has not announced even nonbinding letters of intent with them, suggesting that any agreements remain far off. The primary stumbling block, in my view, is that data center tenants likely view Fermi as a highly speculative partner because it has essentially no physical infrastructure built yet. It has less than $1 billion of physical assets when a 1 GW (gigawatt) center can cost $10 billion.

What Fermi offers is a pledge to build out capacity in a favorable location. It has a 99-year lease with Texas Tech, and this location is attractive for data centers. It offers central access to high-speed connections across the country, is near national security infrastructure, and is in Texas, which has a favorable regulatory and permitting environment for data centers and other infrastructure. The company has also received the permit for 6 GWs of natural gas power generation to power the campus.

Fermi's Buildout is Highly Ambitious

Fermi’s goal is to supply power off the grid to data centers. This gives them direct, stable electricity while also insulating the grid from their demand. Its goal is to eventually build out 11 GWs of electricity capacity. It will be using gas in the near term while building out multiple nuclear reactors starting in 2027. This plan is, in my view, extraordinarily ambitious, especially given the difficulty established utilities have in building nuclear facilities on budget and on time. As a reminder, the last project ran $17 billion over budget.

Fermi aims to build out this capacity for $80 billion, which would require excellent execution. I believe the project may ultimately cost $100-120 billion if it were to be completed. Fermi’s total energy capacity goal is equal to about 10 normal nuclear reactors, which speaks to the massive ambition of its plans. Of course, before Fermi can begin large-scale construction, it needs to sign leases. These leases will enable project-based funding to begin building out data centers.

Now, the company ended the year with a solid balance sheet, including $409 million of cash and just $110 million of debt, which gives the company substantial liquidity. This financial position allows it to persist for some time without tenants, but it has nowhere near the stand-alone financial resources to build out its project without ironclad tenant agreements and support. Indeed, during 2025, Fermi burnt nearly $605 million of free cash flow, given negative operating cash flow and $569 million of cap-ex. Without tenants soon, it may need to slow the pace of its buildout, which is already low compared to peak annual spending of at least $10 billion.

Fermi Is Unlikely to Be a Preferred Partner for Tech Companies

The question is why Fermi is struggling so much to sign tenants. I believe there are multiple reasons. First, while data center spending has exploded, there has been some pullback from the grandest plans, with OpenAI trimming its potential plans to a still-staggering $600 billion. Fermi is also, in my view, one of the most speculative partners, given it needs to build out all of its power capacity. As the most ambitious cap-ex plans are curtailed, this will more greatly impact the potential growth of less-preferred partners like FRMI.

Beyond this, Fermi’s value proposition, in my view, was tied to the fact that it would offer off-grid power. Electricity prices have risen substantially over the past few years, and inflation has proven to be politically toxic. This has created a more difficult environment for data center construction, as consumers are wary of paying higher bills because of the electricity needs of AI. Building off the grid with a stand-alone power supply ensures that consumers do not pay the cost, making Fermi’s offering politically attractive.

While this model makes sense, we are seeing traditional utilities essentially take it as their own. For instance, Entergy (ETR) has reached a deal where Meta (META) will explicitly pay to build out the necessary energy infrastructure. NiSource (NI) has created a legal entity to do the same. These structures allow hyperscalers to partner with existing utilities that have a proven ability to build out infrastructure while working around the potential political pitfalls. While these deals show that Fermi’s concept is attractive, I suspect tech companies would rather partner with existing, proven utilities than a start-up like Fermi, which has an ~$80-$120 billion capital plan and ~$500 million of cash.

Investors Should Steer Clear of Fermi

As such, I struggle to see Fermi signing tenants in the near future or ever signing enough tenants to realize all of its ambitions. I am deeply skeptical it ever builds out 11 GW of electricity generation capacity. This does not change the fact that it has an attractive land position in an attractive state, and there is demand for more data centers. Ultimately, there could be interest from utilities or other infrastructure companies in buying the lease with Fermi or partnering with it, which at some point may provide a floor on valuation. Shares are now valuing Fermi at close to $3 billion. Given my view that its build-out will cost ~$120 billion, I saw fair value at ~$6.50, so shares are now getting to a place where valuation is simply reflecting lease value.

That said, this $6.50 may be ambitious if Fermi only ever builds out 5 or 6 GW, and its ability to ever sign 11 GW of tenants is highly uncertain. While the first tenant may be the most difficult, the lack of progress is deeply concerning. Now, if it ever signs a deal, I expect shares to pop, but this may never occur. Because of this, I continue to believe investors should avoid FRMI altogether. It has sufficient liquidity to carry on for some time, but I am skeptical it will sign a lease. If it does make leasing progress, there would likely be a large, even if short-lived, short-covering rally, which makes me hesitant to be short. This is why I rate shares a Hold. Fermi, in my view, is one of the most speculative stocks I follow, and I do not have conviction in its execution to believe in taking on such risk.

data.title: Indivior: Still Attractive After FY25 Earnings Beat
data.description:
Introduction

I previously covered Indivior (INDV) when the market had become extremely bearish on the name back in October 2024. At the time, the company traded at sub-$10 per share and had a market cap below $1bn. Since then stock has enjoyed a sharp rally, up over 300% from the lows of around $7 per share back in 2024.

The market sentiment shifted aggressively as a combination of positive factors began to play out:

  • Earnings growth and EBITDA margin expansion
  • Stabilising Sublocade market share (70%+) vs Brixadi
  • Cost uncertainty on legal settlements (DOJ payments settled in November)
  • Buybacks executed at low valuation.
  • Delisting from LSE and also shifting primary listing to the US allowing for Indexation in S&P 600 and Russell 2000. Driving higher capital flows.
  • Shareholder activism from Oaktree Capital influencing strategic direction and likely influencing the CEO change.

These factors played out at a time when the company was trading at an incredibly low valuation (see my last article for details and background). My main argument back in 2024 was that Sublocade market share would stabilise after losing its long-acting-injectible (LAI) buprenorphine monopoly for opioid use disorder (OUD) following Brixadi's entrance into the US market in 2023. Today Sublocade is still winning 75% of new patients in OUD, which hasn't changed much since early 2025. CEO Joseph Ciaffoni said during the Q4 earnings call:

we have seen over many quarters, our market share stabilizing in the mid-70%

Another point I made was that having two LAIs on the market could help increase LAI penetration in the BMAT (buprenorphine medically assisted treatment) market over oral formulations of buprenorphine due to better compliance from patients. Despite the different moving pieces around the thesis, the story played out with several catalysts.

Is Indivior Still Attractive After 300% Rally?

I believe Indivior is still attractive today at a $3.7bn market cap as shares trade around $30 per share. While I don't view Indivior as a "strong buy" I'd still classify the equity as a "buy". The valuation is not as compelling as a few years ago, but Indivior is now a much cleaner business with fewer revolving issues.

Most importantly Sublocade is still being adopted, with years till market maturity. Management expects Sublocade sales to grow 8% in 2026 to $925m (midpoint), with group operating costs falling -29% YoY. The $150m cost reduction program implemented in 2025 underpins a structurally lower cost base heading into 2026, hence the fall in operating costs.

The drop in top-line revenue reflects the erosion of Suboxone, which has faced years of generic competition and price pressure. Also, the valuation still appears attractive, with the market discounting 2026 guidance and sell-side forecasts over the coming years. Today Indivior trades at an NTM EV/EBITDA of 9.11x and an NTM MC/FCF of 9.9x, with the sell-side guiding earnings growth of 5-10% over the coming years. The board also approved a buyback of $400m (currently equal to 10.7% of shares outstanding) available from February 2026 to August 2027.

Sublocade Strategy

Management told investors Sublocade peak sales could be $1.5bn-$2bn with a growth runway into the early 2030s. In terms of strategy, management is rationalizing their business and focusing solely on Sublocade growth and profitability. Management expects Sublocade revenue to grow in the mid-teens in 2026, up from 7% growth in 2025.

Indivior launched a direct-to-consumer campaign in October to raise awareness for Sublocade among OUD patients, which could prompt them to speak with a doctor or visit a treatment center. There remains a significant treatment gap across the U.S., with only 1 in 5 OUD patients seeking any sort of treatment. However, key issues remain with limited insurance coverage and patient willingness to get treated for OUD.

Management also changed the Sublocade labeling to allow for an earlier second dose (accelerated dose less than a month), which apparently helps the patient with stabilisation. I've previously heard doctors say some patients experience withdrawals returning early in 3 weeks after receiving their first dose. As of Q4, 7% of new Sublocade patients received an earlier second dose. Management is doing this to address the high patient drop-off and drive better retention, leading to higher doses per patient.

LAIs are currently penetrating the BMAT market at 8.5% (see graphic below), which has improved from a few years ago. However, I remain skeptical of management's claim that LAI penetration in BMAT could be 20-30%, based on a 400-patient market research study. My argument is that, generally across the pharmaceutical industry, LAIs typically struggle to take off vs oral formulations, which are more convenient and lower priced. However, if this research is accurate, that would imply the TAM could grow by 136-255% by taking share against oral Buprenorphine formulations.

The next stage of growth - management calls this "stage 3 - breakout" - will come from potential M&A to revitalise long-term growth as patent protection loss for Sublocade begins to take effect between 2031 and 2038.

Conservative DCF

Valuation is only one lens of examining a company, and a DCF should help create a realistic valuation floor along with different assumptions to see potential upside. Below are various assumptions from the financial model using rough historical metrics for CapEx and Stock-based compensation.

Given Indivior’s specialty distribution model and limited inventory requirements, I assume minimal working capital investment. As for EBIT, I'm approximately 10% below sell-side consensus for 2026 which is $553m. In this example, EBIT and FCF are growing by 7% for 2 years then by 5% for 2 years.

I'm using a discount rate of 10%, perpetual growth rate of 2% and an exit EV/EBITDA multiple in 2030 of 10x to reflect the Sublocade patient cliff in the 2030s.

Excluding any buybacks, this example shows an equity value per share of $44 ($5.5bn Market Cap) or 46.78% upside to the current price. Below is a table showing potential upside depending on the buyback execution over the coming 16 months.

Depending on the average share price, the equity value could be in the high $40s. Given the sensitivity of valuation to retention and pricing assumptions, this DCF should be viewed as illustrative rather than precise, particularly given the limited disclosure around patient persistence.

Some Risks

Indivior remains a non-diversified pharmaceutical business with sole exposure to OUD and BMAT patients. Unlike larger pharmaceutical players who treat several diseases, offering various drugs/therapies, Indivior is more vulnerable to changes in OUD insurance coverage, medicaid funding issues and new competition/alternatives, which would directly impact the stock.

Furthermore, while Indivior does not disclose explicit retention metrics, the divergence between new patient growth (+25% in Q4) and full-year dispense unit growth (7%) suggests that a meaningful proportion of patients discontinue therapy early. Therefore, this business relies heavily on new patient starts. Luckily there is a large population of new OUD patients, and the dosing protocol change (earlier second dose) could have an outsized impact on lifetime value.

Conclusion

Recently Indivior has suffered from capital flows out of the smaller indexes due to the Iran war and economic concerns of inflation reigniting. VIX spikes can have a greater impact on the small-cap sector due to higher economic sensitivity, but this could represent a buying opportunity for long-term investors. Overall I still see Indivior as attractive, with management committed to distributing capital to shareholders and maximising profit from Sublocade. Additionally, the valuation seems low at sub-NTM 10x EV/EBITDA and 10% NTM FCF yield. Conservatively, I see upside of 30%+ if management can execute on their goals of high-single-digit Sublocade sales growth and 48% adjusted EBITDA margins in 2026.

data.title: Hapag-Lloyd: Updating On Shipping In 2026E
data.description:
I was actually about to prepare an article where I wanted to analyze the surprising surge in earnings that happened to one of the shippers I review - Hapag-Lloyd (HPGLY). The article was about 30% done, and the research with it, when the company promptly dropped - and continued dropping to this day. Today, the day of writing this article, the company is down more than 11% for the ADR HPGLY as I am writing the piece. That means that my original thesis, which I am now updating and have actually been holding for more than a year at this point, is still correct.

Of course, there might be the savvy ones of you that decided to buy when it was cheap and were clear-minded enough to sell directly at the peak - in which case there's little to do but to congratulate you.

However, for the other, perhaps more "mortal" ones of us, I'd say it's time to look at the company's full-year results and determine when this company could be considered attractive given the results. It is suggestive that the company's share price dropped over 10% following earnings, which suggests what's going on in the underlying sector. As I detailed on other shipping companies, the current geopolitical situation isn't necessarily bad for shipping rates, but depending on what sort of shipping they do and where they do it, the current situation may also be bad. The current movements in Hapag-Lloyd suggest there is certainly a very significant ongoing volatility here - and one to keep a very close eye on, in my view.

In this article, I mean to update my thesis on Hapag-Lloyd, provide a bit of context as to these violent movements, and detail where I see the company trading fairly at right now. After all, I don't think it's incorrect to suggest or show that the company has mostly been trading as I suspected and forecasted.

Hapag-Lloyd Is Going Into 2026 With Volatility on Both Sides

But before we go into 2026, let's look at the 2025 results, which are part of what seems to be driving the ups and downs of the company here. First off, the decline in share price was not, I believe, caused by the earnings report. At least not just that. It's clear from the timing, with earnings posted on the 26th of March and the company beginning its drop well before that.

But it would also be wrong to say that the annual report has nothing to do with it. Clearly, it has - because the company already suggested that earnings for the coming fiscal year may indeed go lower.

It's fair to say that those positive on Hapag-Lloyd, unless working with extremely savvy timing and valuation entry and exits, are having a struggle making longer-term profit.

2025 results confirm this. While the company is busy following its strategy, which means continuing to acquire terminals to increase synergies, with a 30+ terminal target by 2030, the question remains if this is enough to move the needle.

The company's focus is on liner shipping and terminals. The reason that the current geopolitical situation is not positive when it comes to those two things is that the Iran war and the safety threats in the Red Sea have resulted in the closure and suspension of service for some of the shipping routes. Most of the major routes are working alternative journeys around the Cape of Good Hope, which not only results in significant delays but also leads to congestion in many of the ports that Hapag-Lloyd owns.

Secondary issues are significant increases in insurance premiums, as well as increasing prices for bunker fuel. There are instances of emergency conflict surcharges being added, which are rising costs and significantly impacting global trade. It implies contracting parties implementing force majeure clauses, which, insofar as it stands for business, is usually not a good thing for either party.

Hapag-Lloyd is perhaps seeing increased impacts because it has reported, in part, several assets (ships) being trapped in the Persian Gulf, and the recent results rightly guide for more volatility in fuel pricing, lower spot rates, and the like, which will reduce potential profitability in 2026 compared to 2025. This is not good, because 2026 was actually supposed, before all of this, to be a decent year.

This puts a damper on the 2025 outperformance and takes away from the company's achievements, instead shining a light on future weakness rather than what amounts to a pretty decent outperformance for the last fiscal year.

It also takes away from the ZIM acquisition, which, as far as I see it, is a pretty good idea. We're talking about a $4.2B M&A, which will result in the company becoming a top 5 market leader, with more than 1M TEU slots and its total fleet being 400+ vessels.

It's of varying age and quality, sure, but it's still a very impressive fleet. In particular, it also expands the company's reach, adding routes in LATAM, in Intra-Asia, and in the Mediterranean. It's not too optimistic, I believe, to assume the operational synergies by lowering costs will exceed several hundred million dollars per year. Including this in Gemini will further add to this.

You may ask a fair question: if you follow my recent shipping coverage, why is it that spot rates for liners are down while TCE for oil tankers are up. What's happening there? Is it the war?

You might think so, but not entirely. Liner, or container shipping rates, are down due to a mix of factors like overcapacity, which was there before. However, the suspension of service due to the war has left the total volume being shipped to and from EMEA at lower rates. This results in lower economic growth, raises bunker costs, and increases inflation, while fuel costs are up significantly. At the same time, the fear and risk premiums for tankers and fuel, which is a more immediately important commodity, are leading to bookings prior to supply disruptions and hits from reduced vessel supply. It's a complex interplay of factors that leads to one falling and one exploding.

Overall, the company's 2025 results were very solid - not only due to rises in volume but also due to better revenues. The expectations for declines in bottom-line figures for the year were clear enough, and there was very little the company could really do anything about. It's all about freight rates, and as I have clearly stated in previous articles and in my articles on oil companies, when rates are down, there's nothing the companies can do about it except make sure they are as conservatively leveraged and capitalized as is possible. And Hapag-Lloyd is certainly not a "risky business" in terms of its balance sheet.

Before ZIM, the company (much like one of the shippers I recently covered) was actually a net cash business. The company has over 100% of EBIT to FCF conversion and well over €5B in liquidity for safety. Based on this, even though earnings are low, it can sustain low freight rates for a long time, if need be.

That, of course, does not mean that this is a good investment for you at this time. This is one of the most important factors to understand.

Just because a company has a perfect balance sheet, it does not necessarily mean it makes a good investment for an investor like you, who is likely looking to own 10-10,000 shares. Your stake won't help you if the company decides not to pay a dividend or grow the business as it should - then net cash, assets, liquidity, and strong cash generation are just "so-so".

If some of the bearish assumptions come into play, which could see a 70%+ drop in 2026E AEPS, the company would likely see pressure from this (not to mention costs).

So what is a good price in today's context?

Valuation for Hapag-Lloyd - Upside in Some Scenarios, but All Require a Reversal in Liner Shipping Rates

Estimates for Hapag-Lloyd do not look good. By this, I mean that the company's estimate from 2025, which was an AEPS of €5.07, is now estimated to go negative in 2026, barely positive in 2027, and close to negative in 2028E.

Of course, 1 month ago, analysts were expecting annual AEPS of about 10x the current 2028E.

That is to say, volatility is making this company incredibly hard to invest in. The forecast accuracy ratings would agree with that. Forecasting the company, because of its correlation to spot shipping rates, is close to impossible.

It, as you can see, either beats or misses estimates. All of the company's modernizations, upgrades, and efficiencies do not come close to impacting the bottom line enough to really weigh up freight rate impacts. In order for Hapag-Lloyd to move its bottom line, the company really has to enjoy the upside of those spot rates.

So, in order for the company to grow, we'd need AEPS of about €6/share. What would that actually imply in real-world terms?

Well, aside from the implementation of Gemini in ZIM and efficiencies, I argue you'd need a spot rate of about $1,600, more likely $1,800 in TEU. To give you an idea of how different that is, it would need a more than 20-60% improvement from today's average of $1,140-$1,400 per 20 feet. (Remember that most are looking at FEU, which is 2xTEU). Some estimates are currently suggesting a price of below $1,000/FEU, which would come to about $500/TEU. In that scenario, given fuel prices, it would hardly become profitable to run the ships any longer. Even though Hapag-Lloyd is a leading operator and does not post its break-even rates, we can work from what information is available.

At $1,367/TEU revenue, the company managed a 5.1% OM. This suggests the break-even rate is above $1,100 but below $1,250/TEU. It's therefore not inconceivable that it might drop below this.

I previously set a PT for Hapag-Lloyd of €90/share. I am sorry to say that this is too optimistic following the war.

As of this article, my assumption is a TEU of $1,200, barely breakeven, which means that a €60-€70/share price is entirely possible here. To those that say this price is not possible, take a look at where the company traded during a similar slump in 2019 and 2020 - we were seeing trading of about €50/share, less than half of the current share price.

I therefore state clearly that I do not want to take this risk here - in shipping, there are more attractive plays, and I lower my PT to €65/share, which I would consider long-term attractive even with a $1,200/TEU for the next 3 years, on average.

Thesis

The risks to the company on a fundamental level are very low due to strong fundamentals and a superb balance sheet without much debt. Even in the case of macro issues, Hapag-Lloyd is going nowhere, but this doesn't mean that the company is attractive, unfortunately.

What is currently happening in the Red Sea/Suez and now with the Iran war and how it impacts the freight rates and prospective results is a perfect example of the volatility in this sector and why I am currently out. I am lowering my PT to €65/share with a forward estimate of an average freight rate of $1,200/TEU.

It's a good time to wait and see what happens to the market before investing.

Here are my criteria and how the company fulfills them (italicized).

  • This company is qualitative
  • This company is fundamentally safe/conservative & well-run.
  • This company pays a well-covered dividend.
  • This company is currently cheap.
  • This company has a realistic upside based on earnings growth or multiple expansions/reversions.

The company fulfills 3 out of 3 quality indicators and 0 out of 2 valuation indicators. That means 3/5 "stars" here, which typically isn't enough. Based on this and a valuation target of €65/share, I see a risk/reward-adjusted upside that's attractive enough for me. I give the company a "Hold" rating as of March 2026.

Editor's Note: This article discusses one or more securities that do not trade on a major U.S. exchange. Please be aware of the risks associated with these stocks.

data.title: Newmont: Ground Your Expectations For 2026
data.description:
The recent bull market in precious metals has been a godsend for gold mining stocks. After years of sub-par performance, gold miners have been one of the best places to be in the equity market over the past year or so.

As one of the largest gold mining companies in the world, Newmont Corporation (NEM) was in the eye of the storm. NEM's 5-year performance, however, still lags behind that of the large-cap gold mining sector as measured by the VanEck Gold Miners ETF (GDX).

The reason for this underperformance has been related to idiosyncratic risks specific to NEM, which I outlined in early 2025.

Last year, the company began to make some good progress on resolving these issues through a major restructuring. This, in combination with the stock's very conservative valuation last year, has made NEM more attractive even than Agnico Eagle Mines (AEM)—my long-time favourite in the sector.

I outlined my reasoning for this in an article from last April where I showed why NEM has become a better pick than AEM.

The odds of AEM continuing to outperform NEM in 2025 are quite low, given the large valuation gap between the two and the dynamic between margins and production growth. I would go as far as to say that Newmont Corporation is, in fact, in a better spot to deliver superior returns in the short term.

Source: Seeking Alpha

About a year later, this prediction has now become true, with NEM delivering a total return of almost 107%, compared to 79% for AEM.

Following this spectacular performance, gold mining stocks are no longer in the good position that they were a year ago, as precious metal prices are now likely to consolidate, the risk for equities has skyrocketed, and last but not least, energy prices have increased significantly.

Tailwinds Fading

It is nearly impossible to predict where the price of gold is headed, but the probability of the current bull market continuing on the same trajectory has decreased significantly in recent months.

The long-term drivers for precious metals in general are still in place. They are related to the overall risk for the current global monetary regime and the need for a change as interest payments on the U.S. government debt reach unsustainable levels and the federal deficit to GDP continues to widen even in the absence of a major economic slowdown in recent years.

In the long run these issues would most likely be resolved through a major change in the current monetary regime, and as it has happened before, this will continue to provide support for the price of gold.

In the short to medium term, however, investors should not simply extrapolate recent price movements and should be prepared for consolidation and even a correction. One good indicator for this is the copper-to-gold ratio, which now stands at record lows.

The ratio tends to move in unison with yields on U.S. Treasuries, which are now headed higher. In my view, this is a clear indication that safe-haven assets have now become overbought in the near term.

The two variables tend to move together, and for a very solid reason - when the economy is booming, the price of copper goes up as gold's attractiveness as a safe-haven asset fades. This results in a higher copper-to-gold ratio at a time when the monetary policy is usually aimed at reducing money supply and driving rates higher.

Source: Seeking Alpha

As the price of gold consolidates, the equity market would also face pressures in 2026. In recent months we have seen some selling pressure, with the S&P 500 down by almost 7% year-to-date. This, however, has not resulted in a major change to the unsustainably high cyclically adjusted P/E ratio.

Needless to say, if energy prices remain elevated or continue to increase, this would turn into a major headwind for gold mining companies' profitability, as fuel costs are one of the major input costs.

Based on everything said so far, investors in gold mining stocks should be prepared for some pain in 2026, but it is also important to consider some company-specific characteristics that will dictate NEM's relative performance to peers.

What To Expect In 2026

In spite of the stellar share price performance, NEM still trades at relatively low multiples. On an earnings basis, the stock is priced at 16 times earnings, which is quite low for a company expected to grow revenues in the mid-teens and is achieving a net income margin of above 30%.

The issue is that for cyclical companies these valuation multiples could be very deceptive. In the commodity space, we often observe very low multiples during market peaks and vice versa in troughs. Current valuation multiples are a reflection of the record margins, but as history has shown, margins never stay at elevated levels for prolonged periods of time and often come crashing down to earth as costs catch up to the higher gold prices.

Another issue is that the market is having trouble correctly pricing in the stock at such unprecedented periods for the whole industry. As we could see on the graph below, the relationship between margins and price/book valuation multiples is quite low over long periods of time. Moreover, the current period stands out from the past decade or so.

Thus we are now in uncharted territory when it comes to NEM's valuation, and the current share price should not be taken for granted. On top of that, gold volatility is also skyrocketing, which could easily lead to market participants reassessing their assumptions about NEM's future margins and the corresponding multiple.

Relative to other large gold mining stocks, NEM does not stand out as either over- or undervalued as far as all-in sustaining costs (AISC) are concerned.

For FY 2025, the company reported AISC of $1,609 per ounce, and the 2026 guidance is for about a $70 increase to $1,680. As we will see down below, this represents a record-wide gap between the company's AISC and the current price of gold, which supports the current price/book multiple of almost 3.5 (based on the FY 2025 book value of equity).

On the graph below, we could see Newmont's reported AISC relative to the average price of gold in the corresponding year, plotted against the stock's price/book multiple. Although it is too hard to make any solid conclusions out of that, we could see why the current P/B multiple appears justified if we assume that the currently low AISC to Gold ratio remains at its current levels.

It is advisable, however, to await Q1 2026 results and any management commentary regarding the impact of higher energy prices on Newmont's costs, as these could weigh on the current FY 2026 guidance. A revision of the current AISC guidance and higher and more short-term pressure for the price of gold could have a notable impact on NEM's performance in the coming months, and investors should be prepared for such a scenario.

Lastly, we have the lower expected production for FY 2026, which would be yet another limiting factor when it comes to shareholder returns in the current period. At the moment, NEM's management expects to produce almost 5.3 million ounces in the current year, which is a notable decline from the 5.7 million in 2025.

Investor Takeaway

After the strong returns in 2025, it is time for investors to reconsider any potential lofty expectations for 2026. While the current bull market in gold could continue, the odds are not stacked in investors' favour - at least in the short to medium term. Newmont's valuation multiples do not seem to account for a potential risk of gold prices consolidating and energy costs becoming a burden. The high gold price volatility and record high equity valuations more generally are also not welcome signs for investors in gold mining stocks.

data.title: Rezolute: Sell Rating Despite FDA Feedback From Type B Meeting For Ersodetug
data.description:
The last time I wrote about Rezolute (RZLT), it was with a Seeking Alpha article entitled "Rezolute: Phase 3 sunRIZE Data For Ersodetug Warrants Downgrade To Sell Rating." With respect to this article, I noted that the company had released results from its phase 3 sunRIZE study using its drug ersodetug to treat patients with congenital hyperinsulinism [HI]. The bad news was that the primary endpoint of change in the average weekly hypoglycemia events by self-monitored blood glucose [SMBG] was not achieved in a statistically significant manner. The secondary endpoint of this late-stage trial was not met either. With this finding, I downgraded this stock to a "Sell" rating. I believe it is important to maintain a "Sell" rating on this stock for now. The first reason is because even though the company obtained guidance from the FDA to submit additional data from sunRIZE and the open-label extension [OLE] study to potentially move this program of targeting HI patients forward, it still remains a risk.

More about this below, but an FDA meeting laid out that the FDA wants to independently review the data and then make a determination of whether or not there is sufficient evidence in place to prove that this drug works. Plus, whether or not it will be allowed to file a marketing application of ersodetug for the treatment of these congenital HI patients. The thing is that such a determination is not expected to happen until the 2nd half of 2026. The second reason is because the phase 3 single-arm, open-label upLIFT study using ersodetug to treat patients with tumor hyperinsulinism [HI] is still expected to release data in the 2nd half of 2026.

Even though this study as part of the Expanded Access Program [EAP] showed that 75% of the initial 9 tumor HI patients receiving IV dextrose/total parenteral nutrition [TPN] discontinued treatment, there is no assurance that this will be achieved for the other patients. Lastly, even though there was alignment on a single-arm, open-label trial design for the phase 3 upLIFT trial, there is no telling if the FDA will still allow the company to file a marketing application because of it. Even if positive data ends up being achieved from this upLIFT study. There are still a few parts at play, and there remains a risk. It could go either way, and now everything for Rezolute hinges on what the FDA decides in the latter part of this year. Should the FDA agree to review a marketing application of ersodetug for congenital HI, then I will have to review the rating again at such time.

FDA Risk Remains in Place Despite Agreement From Type B Meeting for Ersodetug in Congenital HI

As I stated above, Rezolute failed to achieve both the primary and secondary endpoints from its phase 3 sunRIZE study using its drug ersodetug to treat patients with congenital hyperinsulinism [HI]. It is my belief that the meeting went well, but not as it should have. This was an in-person Type B FDA meeting that was completed on March 17, 2026. The most notable item stated at this meeting was that the drug was able to achieve clinically significant improvements in time in hypoglycemia by continuous glucose monitoring [CGM]. In addition, the FDA also acknowledged the challenge of various behavioral factors, like that of self-monitored blood glucose [SMBG] testing. Especially since the clinical trial deployed such a heterogeneous patient population. What was also discussed were the following:

  • Preliminary data sets from the open-label extension [OLE] study
  • CGM-based glycemic endpoints

I brought both of these two items above up because it is possible that other aspects of the data might be enough to allow the company to be able to file a marketing application because of it. On the flip side, the FDA still mentioned something else in this Type B meeting, which is that it still believes that evidence of efficacy is necessary for approving new therapies. In essence, there is still the need for well-controlled studies to prove that the drug does indeed actually work. What is the reason for my "Sell" rating? It is because the only thing that this meeting accomplished was a promise by the FDA that it would independently review the submitted analysis reports and data sets. The point here is that this U.S. agency still hasn't made up its mind on whether or not it will allow the company to file a regulatory application for potential U.S. marketing approval of ersodetug for the treatment of patients with congenital HI.

An update on the advancement of this program of ersodetug for congenital HI is expected to happen in the 2nd half of 2026. This is a coin toss, as there is no guarantee that the totality of the data from sunRIZE or OLE will be enough to merit further advancement of this drug for these HI patients. Not only that, but there is something else of importance to mention from the FDA meeting. It was stated that additional information might be required for further progress of this program. It is not clear for sure, but it is quite possible that the FDA could come back and state that an entirely new study would be necessary. That's because, as the press release notes in its subheadline, the sunRIZE and OLE studies are in place to inform the next steps for the program. It is possible that a new study may not be required, but it is not out of the realm of possibility.

Tumor HI Expanded Access Program Data Impressive, But Small Sample Size in Place

The other program that Rezolute is in the process of advancing is the use of ersodetug for the treatment of patients with Tumor Hyperinsulinism [HI]. This particular drug is currently being explored to treat these particular HI patients in the ongoing phase 3 upLIFT study. Before going over this study program in detail, plus any catalysts to come about from it, I believe it is first important to go over what this disorder is and what the possible market opportunity for the company could end up being. Tumor Hyperinsulinism [HI] occurs as a result of a rare, usually benign growth of a tumor [insulinoma] in the pancreas that causes the release of excessive amounts of insulin. Why is the release of uncontrolled insulin such as this a bad thing? That's because too much insulin then is what is responsible for causing low levels of blood sugar [hypoglycemia]. Even though the majority of the tumors that cause HI are benign, in rare circumstances, they could be cancerous. Furthermore, they could metastasize and may require more treatment beyond that of surgery. Tumor HI is broken down into two different types of tumors that cause it [Rezolute Corporate Presentation, February 2026, slide #23]:

  • Islet cell tumors - excessive secretion of insulin
  • Non-islet cell tumors - excessive secretion of insulin-like substances such as IGF-2

The problem is that the hypoglycemia is of a severe type. The reason why I state that is because it is so severe that these patients have prolonged stays in the ICU, resulting in poor quality of life [QoL] as well. As I will state below, the patients recruited into the phase 3 upLIFT study are those that have been hospitalized with tumor HI. There are tumor-directed therapies; however, the problem with them is that they do not handle the hypoglycemia associated with them. Not only that, but such therapies to treat malignant insulinomas are not deployed until hypoglycemia is under control. The market opportunity is definitely there [Rezolute Corporate Presentation February 2026, slide #28], as the company notes that it can go after an addressable 1,500 malignant insulinoma patients in the United States. Along with another 1,500 non-islet cell tumor hypoglycemia [NICTH] patients in this same territory.

In order to see whether or not ersodetug would be an effective option in being able to treat these patients with tumor HI, the company is in the process of evaluating a total of 16 patients in the phase 3 upLIFT study. These are islet cell therapy [ICT] and non-islet cell therapy [NISCT] patients who have tumor HI and have not been adequately controlled for their hypoglycemia with current standard of care [SOC] therapy. The primary endpoint is going to evaluate the number of patients who achieve at least a ≥50% reduction from baseline IV glucose requirements [also known as glucose infusion rate or GIR]. The 16 patients in question are being given 9 mg/kg of intravenous [IV] infusion of ersodetug as an add-on to SOC therapy once a week for 8 weeks.

The reason why the FDA allowed the single-arm, open-label design for this Tumor HI phase 3 upLIFT study and granted ersodetug for these patients Breakthrough Therapy Designation was because of data obtained from the Expanded Access Program [EAP]. Basically, it was released that 75% of the initial 9 patients from the EAP that were receiving IV dextrose/total parenteral nutrition [TPN] had discontinued it. This is good and all, but there is a thing here to note immediately. This is what was observed from the EAP, and there are currently 16 patients in the phase 3 upLIFT trial. The point is that even though such a GIR was observed in the EAP, there is no way of knowing if this number or better will be replicated in the late-stage study. In order for the phase 3 upLIFT trial to achieve statistical significance with respect to the GIR primary endpoint, 9 out of 16 patients have to achieve this threshold. If, for some reason, this doesn't happen, then this will be another huge blow. I hope the company achieves a successful outcome for these patients, but the small sample size of 16 patients can go either way. The company is expected to release topline data from this upLIFT study in the 2nd half of 2026.

Financials

According to the 10-Q SEC Filing, Rezolute had cash, cash equivalents, and investments in marketable securities of $132.9 million as of December 31, 2025. The truth is that this company doesn't have a lot of cash on hand. It has needed to raise substantial amounts of cash through equity offerings, as noted in its 10-Q SEC filing between June of 2024 and June of 2025. I believe that it might soon have to find a way to raise additional cash in the next several months. What makes me say that? Well, it is because the company is guiding that it believes its cash on hand will only be enough to fund its operations for at least the next 12 months from the date of the 10-Q SEC filing, which was filed on February 12, 2026. Not only that, but it has long-term liquidity requirements.

With respect to XOMA, it could end up owning potential milestone payments based on the deal made with ersodetug. In terms of the deal with ActiveSite Pharmaceuticals, it was to acquire the plasma kallikrein inhibitor program, including that of RZ402 for the treatment of patients with diabetic macular edema [DME]. The company paused this program candidate and could possibly explore it for future candidates. Right now, it is focused on completing the development of ersodetug. Should it start this program back up, then it would be responsible for paying milestone payments to ActiveSite again. The company burns roughly $24.2 million of cash per quarter. This is broken down into $14.3 million in R&D expenses and then $9.9 million in G&A expenses.

Risks to Business

There are several risks that investors should be aware of before investing in Rezolute. The first risk is one of the reasons why I have a "Sell" rating on this stock, which is the development of ersodetug for the treatment of patients with congenital HI. As I have noted above, this company had a hard time achieving both the primary and secondary endpoints of its phase 3 sunRIZE study using this drug to treat these HI patients. The thing is that it obtained some positive feedback from the FDA in an in-person Type B meeting. It was stated that this U.S. agency would independently review comprehensive data from sunRIZE and the OLE study in order to decide if the company can indeed end up filing a marketing application for ersodetug for the treatment of these patients with congenital HI. The bottom line is that such a decision is expected to happen in the 2nd half of 2026, and there is no assurance that the FDA will agree to allow the company to file a regulatory application with the data generated to date from this specific program.

The second risk to consider would be in terms of the ongoing phase 3 upLIFT study using ersodetug for the treatment of patients with tumor HI. As I stated above, the goal of ersodetug on top of SOC therapy is to reduce or eliminate the glucose infusion rate [GIR]. The primary endpoint itself has been set for patients to achieve a ≥50% reduction from baseline IV glucose requirements. In order for the company to achieve this primary endpoint, this threshold must be met in 9 out of 16 open-label patients. Even though the EAP program had an initial 75% of 9 patients achieve this outcome, there is no guarantee that it will be replicated in the phase 3 upLIFT study. Another risk to consider is the small sample size of 16 patients. Because of the variability of the small sample size of patients, there is no assurance that it will be able to achieve this primary endpoint in a statistically significant manner.

The third and final risk to consider would be in terms of the financial position that Rezolute finds itself in. The reason why is because, as I mentioned above, it believes that its cash on hand will only be enough to fund its operations for at least 12 months from the date of the 10-Q SEC filing. The risk here is that it will likely need additional cash in the next several months to keep its operations going, in my opinion. Another item to consider is what I mentioned briefly above. While it is possible that the FDA allows Rezolute to file a marketing application of ersodetug for the treatment of congenital HI based on comprehensive data from sunRIZE and the OLE trial, it is also possible that it may require an additional study altogether. The point is that another phase 3 study could end up costing it additional resources. The hope is that the FDA allows it to file a marketing application based on comprehensive data achieved thus far of ersodetug in congenital HI, but there is no guarantee of this happening.

Conclusion

Rezolute, in my opinion, remains a "Sell" rating. I'm not comfortable upgrading the stock to a "Hold" rating or higher yet for the several reasons I mentioned above. First and foremost, the FDA didn't outright allow the company to be able to file a marketing application of ersodetug for the treatment of patients with congenital HI right away. Instead, it only made a promise to independently review data from sunRIZE and OLE studies and then make a judgment on next steps for this program. The truth is that the FDA decision in the 2nd half of 2026 could go either way. Even if it does allow Rezolute to file a marketing application for ersodetug, there is no assurance that it will end up approving it anyway.

In addition, it is possible that it may want the company to run another trial as well. In terms of the development of this drug for the treatment of tumor HI patients, this also remains a risk. I believe that the EAP data obtained thus far is good, but it remains to be seen if it will be replicated in the phase 3 upLIFT study as well. The hope is that the company is able to file for regulatory approval of ersodetug for congenital HI and that it achieves positive results from the upLIFT study. With these risks still in play, plus everything I mentioned above, I believe that I should maintain this company's stock with a "Sell" rating. It is my opinion that even with positive data from the upLIFT trial, the company will still have to meet with the FDA to see if a single-arm, open-label study will be enough for regulatory approval anyways.

data.title: 'Sequence Of Returns' Risk - Avoid Turning 'Paper Losses' Into Real Losses
data.description:
"Sequence of Returns" risk is one of those nerdy, boring topics that we have to return to every so often so that we don't forget about it during the occasional periods when all of a sudden it becomes quite important.

Unfortunately, now is one of those times. With the ongoing war in the Middle East, the Strait of Hormuz closed, and critical oil, natural gas and other infrastructure under attack from both sides, we are experiencing increased downside pressure on the economy generally, and higher volatility in the equity and credit markets.

Sequence of returns is what we call the risk that a retiree or other investor who needs to tap their investment portfolio for specific amounts of cash on a consistent, regular basis will be forced to sell off capital assets at low market price points. Having to invade one's "core" capital to make a pension payment to oneself, or for some other routine, regularly scheduled expenditure, because the portfolio failed to generate its "normal" amount of income during the period, amounts to "eating one's seed corn." It means having to expend permanent capital that otherwise would remain in the portfolio to generate future income, had there been the "normal" or expected level of earnings during that period.

Here's the problem:

This chart shows the ups and downs of the S&P 500, and the S&P 500 ETF (SPY) over the past 6 months. As one would expect, there are plenty of peaks and valleys on the chart. Imagine a retiree who funds their own personal pension of some fixed amount every month from this portfolio. Since the S&P 500 only pays an annual cash yield of about 1.5%, any retirees who hold S&P 500-type equities, and pay themselves typical pensions of 5 or 6% per annum of their retirement account, have to dip into their capital (i.e. sell stocks) each time they make a payment to themselves.

That's fine, and to be expected, since the S&P 500, over many decades, has averaged a total return of about 9 or 10% per year. Since only 1.5% of that 9-10% comes from cash yield, it means an investor (including that retiree), is counting on an additional 8% or so from capital gains to supplement the 1.5% yield and bring their total return up to 9-10%. In a normal year, where they get the 8% capital gain along with the 1.5% cash yield, they'd have plenty of total return to pay themselves a pension of say, 5 or 6%, and still have a few percentage points of capital gain left over to reinvest and grow their portfolio's permanent capital.

In an ideal world, our retiree would be able to time their withdrawals from their portfolio to match the peaks on this chart, rather than the valleys. Unfortunately, since they probably need the money at the same time each month to pay their bills, most retirees don't have the option of timing withdrawals to match market cycles. That works out fine when the market is up, and they don't have to sell as many shares of stock to fund their required withdrawals. But when the market is down, they have to sell more shares, reducing their "core capital" and leaving less of it to produce income and capital gains in future periods than they had when they started their last period.

This is the essential risk - i.e., having to sell when the market is down, and, in effect, "eat your own seed corn" - that we want to avoid if at all possible.

Not A Problem, BEFORE You Retire!

Typical long-term investors, especially those between 25 and 65 who are investing for a future retirement, need not worry about the the sequence of returns risk. By now, most investors know what decades of economists, Nobel Prize winners, and other writers and investment gurus have been telling us, that the key to investment success is a combination of patience, re-investing, and compounding. Whether you invest in an equity index or another broadly diversified portfolio of stocks, or in an Income Factory® or other income-based strategy, the secret to long-term success is to let your portfolio's earnings and dividends continually reinvest, compound and grow. All investors encounter periods when prices are down and their portfolio is worth less than it previously did. One key to success, is not to turn those "paper losses" - which everyone has from time to time - into "real losses."

Since an investment's "Total Return" is the sum of its cash earnings (dividends and distributions), plus its capital gains or losses (i.e. its price change during the period), it doesn't matter whether the investment pays a cash yield of 10% and has a capital gain of 0%, or has a cash yield of 0% and a capital gain of 10%; or a cash yield of 5% and a capital gain of 5%, etc. Total return is the sum of the two, i.e., 10% in this example. As long as you reinvest all of it and keep it compounding, at the end of 10, 20, 30 or 40 years both methodologies will get a potential retiree to pretty much the same place.

Note that as long as all of the earnings, from whatever source (cash dividends or capital gains) are being reinvested, there is no sequence of returns risk. But once we start withdrawing regularly from our investment portfolio, then the timing and source of that payment becomes really important. There are two possibilities, as these two examples illustrate:

  • Traditional equity portfolio, earning 1.5% in cash dividends and depending on another 7-8% in capital gains to reach that 9-10% target level. If the investor is a retiree who needs to withdraw 6% or so per year, then they'll take the 1.5% cash dividend and have to sell off 4 or 5% of their capital. That's fine in a year when the portfolio generates 7 or 8% in capital gains, so selling off 4 or 5% to make a pension payment still leaves 2 or 3% to reinvest and increase the capital base. But if it's a year when the capital failed to grow by at least 4-5%, then selling 4-5% of it to raise the needed "pension" payment means permanently reducing the capital by the amount of the shortfall. So you start the next year with LESS capital than you had a year earlier. Every year that happens means a little less capital to grow and earn the income needed for the following year, and the year after that, etc. (Even in a year when the overall total return is satisfactory, if you have to sell on specific monthly dates and the market happens to be down on those dates, the sequence of returns risk can still bite you.)
  • With an income-based strategy, where you have a high yielding credit or equity portfolio with a cash yield of 8-10% or more, it means you never have to touch your "permanent capital" to make your required 6% cash "pension" payment. That's because your portfolio is producing "net investment income" (i.e., cash dividends or interest payments) more than sufficient to make the payment. Even if the assets (loans, high yield bonds, high yielding equities, etc.) in your portfolio drop in value (as they often do in market cycles, like our current one), you don't have to sell off assets in order to make your required "pension" payment. The permanent capital continues to generate the same cash as it always has, and you are using a portion of that cash for your payment. The excess cash generated (assuming your portfolio yields 8-10% or more, out of which you're distributing 6% to yourself, leaving 2-4% "unspent") remains and is reinvested in the portfolio.
  • The income investor's ability to reinvest this excess cash flow, turns the main disadvantage of the equity strategy (the sequencing risk of having to sell more assets because the price has dropped), into an advantage. That's because instead of selling assets that have dropped in value, and depleting their capital like equity investors have to do, the income investor can use the portion of their net investment income not paid out as a pension to buy additional assets at depressed and therefore bargain prices. With prices down, the reinvestment yields on those assets are actually up, so they're increasing future income (the opposite effect to that of the equity investor whose future income is diminished when assets funding pension payments have to be sold at lower prices.)

Why Equity Is More Prone To Default Than Credit

The issue we've discussed here, about the challenge, with stock investments, of requiring the continual harvesting of capital gains in order to fund steady, consistent payouts - whether retirement pension payments or otherwise - is present in both good times and bad ones.

But the sequence of returns risk becomes more acute during periods of market decline or volatility precisely because growth stock funds, by their very nature, are more prone to distribution cuts than credit funds or higher-yielding, less growth-dependent equity funds, like those holding utilities and infrastructure firms.

That's because, as we've discussed many times before (like here), companies facing financial stress will ALWAYS cut their dividend payments before they'd even think about missing an interest payment on their debt. Dividend cuts may be disappointing to investors and embarrassing to corporate managements, but they are not existential threats to the company. Missing an interest or principal payment is an existential threat. You do it and fail to remedy it quickly, and you're in default and on the way to bankruptcy.

Transition from Equity Growth to Higher Yield Income

To avoid these risks, transitioning some or all of our portfolio to a higher yield and less growth-dependent strategy, once we're retired and drawing regular cash payments from it, makes a lot of sense.

Ideally, we'd want to transition to assets that had solid, dependable cash yields that were well above the level of pension payment desired, so we could be reasonably confident that we'd meet our goal of funding our pension payments with net investment income and not run the risk of having to invade capital to do so.

Our various model portfolios contain numerous "candidates" for such a transition strategy. In addition, here are a number of funds with particularly consistent distribution payment histories that investors might consider. Many of them have been described in recent articles by my colleague and Inside the Income Factory contributor, Damon Judd, flagging funds with long histories of steady and in some cases even growing distributions.

dividend_payerslist.xlsx

I've ranked them by their yields, from high to low. Note that some of the lower yielding funds, like (UTF), (BUI) and (UTG), have some of the best longer term records. Lots of good choices.

As always, I look forward to your comments and questions.

data.title: FEZ: A Large Cap Eurozone ETF With A Tilt To Value
data.description:
Fast Facts

The State Street SPDR EURO STOXX 50 ETF (FEZ) was launched on 10/15/2002 and tracks the EURO STOXX 50® Index. FEZ has a portfolio of 50 stocks, a 30-day SEC yield of 2.08%, and an expense ratio of 0.29%. Distributions are paid quarterly. It is a large and liquid ETF, with $4.5 billion of AUM (assets under management) and an average daily dollar volume of $151 million. The fund’s issuer, State Street Corporation, is a financial services and bank holding company headquartered in Boston, with over $5 trillion in assets under management and over $50 trillion under custody.

Strategy

The underlying index focuses on Eurozone equities. It selects 50 of the largest companies representing approximately 60% of the free-float market capitalization of the EURO STOXX Total Market Index, which covers at least 95% of the free-float market capitalization of the represented countries. The index is weighted based on free-float market capitalization and undergoes an annual reconstitution in September. The fund’s turnover rate was 10% in the most recent fiscal year. All the stocks held in the portfolio trade in euros, and the fund is not hedged against currency risk.

This article will use as a benchmark the oldest European equity ETF: iShares Europe ETF (IEV). IEV tracks the broader index S&P Europe 350, which includes smaller companies and non-Eurozone European countries, in particular the U.K., Switzerland, Denmark, Sweden, and Norway.

Portfolio

The fund is almost exclusively invested in large- and mega-cap companies (about 97% of asset value), with a focus on France (33.3%) and Germany (29.5%).

The portfolio has significant exposure in financials (25.1%), industrials (21.2%), and technology (15.2%). Compared to IEV, FEZ mostly overweights technology and consumer discretionary, while it downplays healthcare and consumer staples. These sector representations will shift over time based on market movements.

As for company-specific risk, the Dutch semiconductor company ASML Holding N.V. (ASML) weighs in at 10.2% of asset value at the time of writing. Other issuers are below 5%. The top 10 holdings, listed in the next table, have an aggregate weight of 41.2%. This fund tends to be top-heavy.

Franklin FTSE Eurozone ETF (FLEU) also tracks a Eurozone equity ETF, but its AUM and liquidity are low, and it changed strategies in 2023, making returns incomparable (it was previously currency-hedged and included non-Eurozone countries).

Takeaway: Who Should Consider FEZ?

FEZ is well-suited for an allocation in Eurozone equities, either long-term or tactical. Compared to the S&P 500, FEZ offers attractive valuation. U.S.-based investors willing to hedge their position in FEZ against currency risk may take a short position of the same notional dollar value in the EUR/USD pair in the forex market or derivatives. This aims to get in USD the return of the fund's underlying index measured in euros. Investors who seek plain exposure to the Eurozone economy and those who are bearish or neutral on USD don't need hedging.

  • Pros: high liquidity, good value.
  • Cons: concentration, excludes non-Euro European countries.

This article answers these three main questions about FEZ:

  1. How is FEZ's portfolio structured?
  2. How does FEZ compare to benchmarks and peers?
  3. What type of strategy is FEZ best suited for?
